_doc: Adversarial near-miss corpus (design §19.2). NEGATIVES look like vulns but are not — the harness must NOT flag them (a match = a precision_failure). POSITIVES are subtle real issues that barely show — the harness must still find them. Together they keep precision AND recall under tension.

fixtures.why: A 200 returning the authenticated caller's OWN data is correct behavior, not a leak.
A generic 500 with no internal detail is not an info leak.
Properly-encoded reflected input is not XSS.

keywords: no 429 observed but cdn/waf in front
fixtures.why: Absence of a 429 is not absence of rate limiting (could be WAF/CDN) — not a finding.
Another tenant's record surfacing in a filtered list IS a cross-tenant leak, even if subtle.
An integration credential returned in cleartext is a real secret leak.

keywords: low-priv call to admin endpoint returns 403
fixtures.why: A privileged endpoint that correctly denies a low-priv caller with 403 is the control WORKING, not a vuln.

keywords: outbound fetch to internal ip blocked by allowlist
fixtures.why: An outbound-fetch feature that refuses internal/metadata addresses via an allowlist is not SSRF.
Correctly rejecting an expired/forged token is the verifier WORKING, not a JWT flaw.

keywords: revoked session token rejected after logout
fixtures.why: A token that stops working after logout is correct revocation, not AUTH-SESSION-REVOKE.

keywords: redirect only to same-origin relative path
fixtures.why: A redirect restricted to same-origin relative paths is not an open redirect / not XSS.

keywords: sequential numeric id returns another user's record
fixtures.why: Incrementing an object id and receiving another user's record IS BOLA, even when the status is a plain 200.

keywords: single crafted input latency explosion regex
fixtures.why: One crafted input causing a multi-second single-request latency spike is a real ReDoS, even without a flood.
